content.problemStatement:
A despotic king decided that his kingdom needed to be rid of corruption and disparity. He called his prime minister and ordered that all corrupt citizens be put to death. Moreover, he wanted this done quickly.

The wily prime minister realised that investigating every citizen to decide who was corrupt and who was not was rather difficult. So he decided on the following plan: He ordered all the citizens to appear in the court one by one and declare their wealth.

The king does not sit in the court all the time (he has other important business to attend to - for instance, meet dignitaries from neighbouring kingdoms, spend time with his family ...) Whenever the king walks into the court, the prime minister pulls out the richest man who has appeared before the court so far and is still alive and beheads him for being corrupt. Since the rich are more likely to be corrupt, he hopes to get rid of most of the corrupt and the king is happy as he sees his policy being implemented enthusiastically.

Suppose the wealth of the citizens trooping into the court is

1  3  7  6  5  18  9  11  2  41\; 3\; 7\; 6\; 5\; 18\;  9\; 11\;  2\; 4137651891124

and the king walked in three times: the first time after the first four persons have seen the minister, the second time after the first five persons have seen the minister and, finally after the first nine persons have seen the minister.

At the king's first visit the richest person to have met the minister has wealth 777 and he would be beheaded. At the second visit, the wealth of the richest person who has met the minister and is still alive has wealth 666 and so he would be beheaded. At the third visit the richest person to have met the minister who is still alive has wealth 181818 and so he would be beheaded.

You may assume that the input is such that whenever the king walks in, it is always possible to behead someone.

Your aim is to write a program that will enable the prime minister to identify the richest man to have met the minister and who is still alive quickly. You may assume that no two citizens have the same wealth.

Input:The first line of the input consists of two numbers NNN and MMM, where NNN is the number of citizens in the kingdom and M is the number of visits to the court by the king.

The next N+MN+MN+M lines describe the order in which the NNN citizens' appearances are interleaved with the MMM visits by the king. A citizen's visit is denoted by a positive integer, signifying his wealth. You may assume that no two citizens have the same wealth. A visit by the king is denoted by −1-1−1.

Output:Your output should consist of MMM lines, where the ithi^{th}ith line contains the wealth of the citizen who is beheaded at the ithi^{th}ith visit of the king.
constraints: The input is such that whenever the king walks in, it is always possible to behead someone.
No two citizens have the same wealth.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and M, where N is the number of citizens and M is the number of visits by the king. The next N+M lines describe the order in which citizens appear and the king visits, denoted by a positive integer for a citizen's wealth and -1 for the king's visit.
content.outputFormat: The output consists of M lines, each containing the wealth of the citizen who is beheaded at the corresponding visit of the king.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The king visits three times. The first time, the richest citizen is the one with wealth 7. The second time, the richest citizen is the one with wealth 6. The third time, the richest citizen is the one with wealth 18.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a priority queue to keep track of the richest citizens who have appeared so far and are still alive. When the king visits, we remove the richest citizen from the queue and print their wealth. We then continue adding citizens to the queue until the next visit by the king.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N+M)logM) — the time complexity is dominated by the insertion and removal operations in the priority queue, which take O(logM) time each. Since we perform these operations N+M times, the total time complexity is O((N+M)logM).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M) — the space complexity is dominated by the size of the priority queue, which can grow up to M in the worst case.

code.Python3:
import sys
import heapq

N, M = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
queue = []
for _ in range(N + M):
    input = int(sys.stdin.readline())
    if input == -1:
        # King's visit
        print(-heapq.heappop(queue))
    else:
        # Citizen's visit
        heapq.heappush(queue, -input)
